Abstract
Venous thromboembolism (VTE) is a frequent complication in patients with multiple myeloma (MM) that leads to increased
morbidity, mortality, treatment interruptions, and reduced quality of life. Αn Expert Panel Consensus Guideline on behalf of the
European Myeloma Network provides evidence‐based recommendations for VTE prevention and treatment in patients
with MM. Key recommendations include the following:
• Patient education. Informing patients about VTE risk and signs/symptoms of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism.
• Specialized care. Establishing a dedicated diagnostic and therapeutic pathway for the prevention and treatment of VTE that
occurs in an outpatient setting.
• Risk assessment. Regular evaluation of VTE risk using validated scores.
• Primary thromboprophylaxis. Initiating pharmacological thromboprophylaxis with low‐molecular‐weight heparin (LMWH) or
specific direct factor Xa inhibitors (anti‐Xa agents) in high‐risk patients.
• Prompt diagnosis and treatment. Early diagnosis of VTE and immediate initiation of antithrombotic therapy with LMWH or anti‐
Xa agents.
• Secondary prophylaxis. Personalized long‐term antithrombotic therapy based on individual profiles of risk of VTE recurrence or
bleeding.
By implementing these guidelines, healthcare providers can improve the prevention and treatment of VTE in patients with MM,
leading to better clinical outcomes and quality of life.

INTRODUCTION
Advances in the treatment of multiple myeloma (MM), including
patients
with
newly
diagnosed
or
relapsed/refractory
disease,
have significantly improved mortality and quality of life. The median
survival of patients with MM has doubled from 3–4 to 8–10 years in
the era of novel therapeutic strategies.1
Venous thromboembolism (VTE), including deep vein throm-
bosis (DVT), pulmonary embolism (PE), and atypical thrombosis
(e.g., cerebral or splanchnic veins), is a common and serious
complication in MM. Its occurrence exacerbates morbidity and
undermines therapeutic gains by complicating treatment regi-
mens, delaying therapy, and increasing mortality risk. The risk of
VTE is driven by a complex interplay of MM‐specific risk factors
(such as hypercoagulability, immunomodulatory drug [IMiD] use,
and disease‐related endothelial dysfunction) and patient‐related
risk factors.
The incidence of VTE in patients with MM ranges between 8
and 22 per 1000 person‐years.2 A competing risk analysis of
13,700 MM patients in the United States revealed a VTE in-
cidence of 1050 events over a 1‐year follow‐up period.2 VTE
rates reported in clinical trials range from 4% to 16%.3,4 Overall,
patients with MM have a ninefold increased risk of VTE compared
to the general population. About 50% of patients present with
symptomatic distal DVT or central venous catheter (CVC)‐related
thrombosis (CRT), whereas 15% suffer a PE. Superficial vein
thrombosis
and
mesenteric
or
upper
limb
vein
thrombosis
have also been reported. In newly diagnosed MM (NDMM)
patients, more than half of the VTE events occur within the first
3–6 months from initial diagnosis and treatment initiation.
Similarly, the risk of VTE significantly increases within the
first
3–6
months
after
documented
treatment
relapse
or
progression.5,6
Monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS), a
precursor/premalignant condition, has also been shown to increase the
risk of VTE.7 In a recent multicenter cohort study of 1491 patients with
MGUS, the incidence of VTE was 1.9 per 1000 person‐years (2.7%
during follow‐up).7 The Iceland Screens, Treats, or Prevents MM
(iSTOPMM) study confirmed that MGUS is associated with a 1.3‐fold
increased risk of VTE (hazard ratio [HR] 1.31; 95% confidence interval
[CI]: 1.08–1.57) compared to healthy controls. After a median follow‐up
of 3.7 years, the rate of VTE was 10.3 per 1000 person‐years in the
MGUS group versus 6.0 per 1000 person‐years in the non‐MGUS
group.8
Large database studies have demonstrated a two‐ to threefold
higher risk of mortality in patients with MM and VTE.9,10 A
recent retrospective study in 672 MM patients who received
lenalidomide‐based triplets or quadruplets suggested a survival
disadvantage for patients who experienced VTE (HR = 2.61,
95% CI = 1.74–3.9). However, disease burden and other risk
factors related with MM confounded this finding.4 Achieving
effective VTE
prevention
and
management
would
improve
quality of life, reduce VTE‐related morbidity, and healthcare
expenditures.11,12
Aiming to contribute to optimal care for patients with MM, this
Expert Panel Consensus on Practice Guidelines for the Prevention and
Treatment of Venous Thromboembolism in Patients with Multiple
Myeloma provides evidence‐based recommendations. These are
tailored to the needs of hematologists, oncologists, internists, an-
giologists, general practitioners, allied health professionals, and
policymakers who are involved in the care of patients with MM. By
implementing these guidelines, physicians can effectively prevent
and treat VTE in MM patients and reduce the overall burden
of VTE.
METHODOLOGY
An interdisciplinary panel of experts on cancer‐associated throm-
bosis (CAT) and experts on MM from different institutions and
countries in Europe, on behalf of the European Myeloma Network
(EMN), developed these recommendations. The guideline devel-
opment
and
structure
followed
the
Population,
Intervention,
Control, and Outcomes (PICO) format. We electronically searched
major databases (i.e., Cochrane Central and MEDLINE, PubMed) to
identify relevant systematic reviews, randomized controlled trials
(RCTs), observational studies, and case series. These electronic
searches were performed looking for studies published in English
from inception to December 2024. For each PICO question, all
potentially eligible studies were assessed for eligibility according to
prespecified criteria. Content experts were asked to indicate any
additional studies not identified by the search (see Supplementary
Material 1).
After the manuscript was prepared, it was discussed at the
EMN Trialist Annual Meeting (Baveno, Italy, November 5–6, 2023
and 2024), followed by two rounds of revisions among the panel
members.
Recommendation formulation. We used the principles outlined in the
evidence to decision (EtD) framework to formulate recommendations.13
The EtD framework addresses several key domains, including priority
setting, the magnitude of benefits and harms, certainty of the evidence,

Primary thromboprophylaxis in patients with MM
Risk factors associated with VTE
The identification of patients at high risk for VTE is critical for the
effective and safe use of pharmacological thromboprophylaxis in
patients with MM. Patients should be assessed for VTE risk at
diagnosis,
at
relapse,
and
systematically
during
treatment.
Patients must be stratified as high‐risk and intermediate/low‐risk
for VTE, enabling targeted prevention strategies that balance the
benefits of pharmacological thromboprophylaxis with the potential
bleeding risk.
Predictors of VTE have been identified from population‐based
databases, registries, hospital records, retrospective cohorts, pro-
spective observational studies, and clinical trials.
The global risk is influenced by the following:
i. Disease‐related risk factors. MM is associated with hypercoa-
gulability and endothelial cell activation. The time since diag-
nosis (NDMM patients are at higher risk of VTE compared to
patients at relapse, as tumor burden is often highest at initial
diagnosis), the tumor burden, immobilization (due to bone
disease or any other reason), levels, and type of para-
proteinemia are predictors of MM‐associated thrombosis.
Monoclonal paraproteinemia may cause hyperviscosity, hy-
perfibrinogenemia, decreased activity of the natural coagula-
tion inhibitors protein C or protein S, acquired resistance to
activated protein C, or impaired fibrinolysis. Cytokines such
as interleukin‐6 and tumor necrosis factor (TNF), as well as
plasma
cell‐derived
extracellular
vesicles,
can
cause
en-
dothelial cell activation and platelet activation; all of which are
associated with the risk of VTE.14–16
ii. Antimyeloma therapy. IMiDs (thalidomide and its derivatives
lenalidomide
and
pomalidomide,
likewise
iberdomide
and
mezigdomide to come in the near future) increase the risk of VTE.
The rate of VTE in patients on thalidomide or lenalidomide
monotherapy
is
3%–4%.
The
addition
of
high‐dose
dex-
amethasone, or multiagent chemotherapy, increases VTE risk up
to 26%.17,18 Although reducing the dose of dexamethasone
lowers the VTE risk, it still remains substantial at around 10%.19
In addition, the risk of VTE remains significantly elevated,
independently of the duration of treatment exposure.20 Pomali-
domide is associated with a lower risk of VTE compared to
lenalidomide, with reported VTE rates being dose‐dependent and
ranging between 3% and 7% in patients receiving a 4‐mg
dose.21 Treatment with the proteasome inhibitor bortezomib is
associated with low VTE risk.22 Carfilzomib induces hypercoa-
gulability and is associated with an increased VTE risk.23 In a
meta‐analysis involving 924 patients treated with carfilzomib/
lenalidomide/dexamethasone (KRd), the VTE rate was 8.9%
versus 6.2% among 1380 patients treated with bortezomib/le-
nalidomide/dexamethasone (VRd).24 The study reviewed one
randomized trial and five retrospective studies; VTE risk assess-
ment using prespecified risk assessment tools was performed

iii. Patient‐related intrinsic risk factors. Advanced age (particularly
in patients older than 70 years), female gender, personal history
of VTE, presence of known hereditary thrombophilia (factor V
Leiden or factor II G20210A mutations, antithrombin, protein C
or protein S deficiency) or other gene polymorphisms (i.e., ABO
locus), cardiovascular risk factors (diabetes, obesity, hyperten-
sion, and dyslipidemias) or cardiovascular disease (ischemic
stroke, coronary artery or carotid artery disease, and peripheral
artery disease), inflammatory bowel disease, autoimmune dis-
eases, and renal insufficiency, figure among the most common
and the most important risk factors for CAT.32
iv. Triggering risk factors. Central vein catheters, use of erythropoietin
or other colony‐stimulating factors, recent hospitalization (less
than 3 months) or hospitalization for acute medical illness, im-
munosuppression, surgical interventions, and immobilization (for
any reason) are among the major triggering risk factors for VTE in
patients with MM.33
Risk assessment models for VTE in patients with MM
Individualized VTE risk evaluation to identify patients eligible
for pharmacological thromboprophylaxis is a clinical need as
recommended by the recent ESMO Clinical Practice Guideline for
the Prevention and Treatment of VTE in Cancer Patients.34 The
guidelines published by the International Myeloma Working Group
(IMWG),35 the EMN,36 and the National Comprehensive Cancer
Network (Oncology Guidelines)37 adopted a simplified algorithm, in practice
since 2014, based on the concept that IMiD‐based therapy is the
primary determinant of VTE risk. Nevertheless, this algorithm does
not stem from a derivation cohort analysis but is based on an
empirical scoring of the VTE risk predictors. According to the
IWMG algorithm, patients are categorized into two risk groups:
low‐risk (0–1 risk factor) and high‐risk (≥2 risk factors) (Table 2).
The IWMG risk stratification is not accurate, leading to an under-
estimation of the VTE risk. A high incidence of VTE in the “low risk”
group of patients has been systematically documented by clinical
studies and case series.38
To improve VTE risk stratification in MM, three validated scoring
systems have emerged: IMPEDE‐VTE, SAVED, and PRISM (Tables 2
and 3). These tools represent significant advances in risk prediction,
though each has distinct clinical applications and limitations, which
are presented herein.
The IMPEDE‐VTE score was derived from analysis of the ret-
rospective database of the Veterans Administration Cancer Registry
including 4446 patients and was validated at the SEER‐Medicare
database.39 The IMPEDE‐VTE score stratifies patients into low‐,
intermediate‐, and high‐risk groups. The reported rates of VTE at
6‐month
follow‐up were
3.3% in
the
low‐risk,
8.3%
in
the
intermediate‐risk, and 15.2% in the high‐risk group. The IMPEDE‐
VTE score showed improved predictive accuracy (c‐statistic = 0.64),
as compared to the IMWG/Oncology Guidelines model (c‐statistic = 0.55). The
IMPEDE‐VTE score was derived from an unselected, real‐world MM
population, making it broadly applicable across diverse clinical
scenarios—including newly diagnosed chemotherapy‐naïve patients,
those undergoing active myeloma treatment, and patients with
disease progression or relapse. This generalizability stems from its
development in heterogeneous cohorts without restrictive enroll-
ment criteria.
The SAVED score was derived from a retrospective database
analysis of 2397 patients treated with IMiDs available at the SEER‐
Medicare database (Tables 2 and 3).40 The SAVED score's applicability
is inherently limited to patients receiving IMiDs, as its derivation cohort
exclusively comprised IMiD‐treated patients. The SAVED score stra-
tifies patients at low‐risk and high‐risk for VTE. The predictive accuracy
of the SAVED score was slightly higher (c‐statistic = 0.6) as compared
to the IMWG/Oncology Guidelines model (c‐statistic = 0.55).
The PRISM score was derived from a retrospective database
of 934 patients treated at the Cleveland Clinic.41 It stratifies
patients into low‐, intermediate‐, and high‐risk groups (Tables 2
and 3). The 12‐month VTE rates were 2.7%, 10.8%, and 36.5%,
respectively. The external validation of the PRISM score in a group
of 257 patients at Columbia University Irving Medical Center
showed a low predicted accuracy (c‐statistic of 0.59), though
slightly improved as compared to the IMWG/Oncology Guidelines algorithm
(c‐statistic = 0.55).
The IMPEDE‐VTE, SAVED, and PRISM scores, as compared to
the
IMWG/Oncology Guidelines
algorithm,
represent
important
advances
in
identifying MM patients at risk for VTE since they have been derived
from well‐defined cohorts and have been validated.
In summary, the comparison of the three scores (Table 2) high-
lights several important points:
i. The IMPEDE‐VTE and PRISM scores can be applied to any patient
with MM, whereas the SAVED score is specifically designed for
patients receiving treatment with IMiDs.
ii. The applicability of the PRISM score is limited by its reliance on
the assessment of metaphase cytogenetics, which are not routi-
nely performed. Moreover, the generalizability of the PRISM
score is questioned since the score is derived from a single‐center
cohort study.

Common limitations across all models and perspectives. All current
RAMs are derived from retrospective cohorts that primarily included
patients treated with older therapeutic regimens, predominantly
doublets, rather than the triplet or quadruplet combinations that are
now standard of care in patients with MM. Additionally, the derivation
cohorts for the IMPEDE‐VTE and SAVED scores mostly involved
patients receiving high‐dose dexamethasone (≥160 mg/cycle), which is
no longer considered standard of care. Newer agents such as
carfilzomib and daratumumab are underrepresented or entirely absent
from these datasets.42
Given these limitations, there is a need to update or recalibrate the
RAMs by evaluating them in cohorts of patients who receive newer an-
timyeloma therapies. There is also a need for improving the predictive
accuracy of the scores. Lastly, the positive predictive value of the current
clinical scores remains modest—approximately 15%—highlighting the
need for more precise stratification tools. Incorporating specific bio-
markers of hypercoagulability offers a promising strategy to enhance the
accuracy of the score and particularly the positive predictive value. On-
going prospective studies are evaluating the clinical utility of biomarkers
such as D‐dimer, thrombin generation, procoagulant phospholipid‐
dependent clotting time, P‐selectin levels, and circulating procoagulant
microvesicles, as well as markers of endothelial cell activation. Integrating
these clinically validated biomarkers with established clinical RAMs is
expected to improve both the accuracy and reliability of VTE risk as-
sessment in patients with MM.
Taking into consideration the ensemble of the above points, and
acknowledging the limitations, among the so far available scores for VTE
risk assessment, the IMPEDE‐VTE score fulfills the minimum criteria for
assessment of all patients with MM, given its comprehensive inclusion of
relevant risk factors, applicability across treatment regimens, reliance on
major available clinical predictors and better accuracy compared either to
the IMWG/Oncology Guidelines or to the SAVED or the PRISM score.
Thromboprophylaxis strategies
Clinical trials designed to evaluate the efficacy and safety of primary
pharmacological thromboprophylaxis in patients with MM are limited.

from clinical studies demonstrates that ASA provides inferior pro-
tection against VTE compared to LMWH, with relative risk (RR) re-
ductions of approximately 50% versus 70%–80%, respectively, in
IMiD‐treated populations.43,44 However, given the established safety
profile and potential benefits, low‐dose aspirin remains a reasonable
thromboprophylaxis option for MM patients classified as low‐risk for
VTE. A meta‐analysis in 439 outpatients with MM showed that
thromboprophylaxis with LMWH significantly reduced the risk of
symptomatic VTE compared to vitamin K antagonists (VKAs) RR 0.33,
95% CI 0.14–0.83; high‐certainty evidence). In 781 patients, LMWH
significantly decreased the rate of symptomatic VTE compared to
ASA (RR 0.51, 95% CI 0.22–1.17; 2 studies, moderate‐certainty evi-
dence).40 Major bleeding was not observed in patients treated with
LMWH or VKA and was reported in less than 1% of patients treated
with ASA.
A more recent systematic review identified only four randomized
clinical trials (RCTs) including in total 1042 patients that evaluated the
efficacy and safety pharmacological thromboprophylaxis in patients
with MM.45 Two studies compared low‐dose ASA to prophylactic
LMWH.46,47 One study evaluated VKA versus LMWH or ASA, and
the fourth compared low‐dose ASA versus direct anti‐Xa agents.3,48
The certainty of evidence comparing the efficacy and safety of ASA,
VKA, and LMWH was low to very low. This uncertainty may stem
from the limited accuracy of the risk assessment models (RAMs) used
for patient stratification and assignment of thromboprophylactic re-
gimens. These methodological shortcomings likely contributed to the
high heterogeneity across studies and the inability to demonstrate a
clear therapeutic superiority of one intervention over another.
A systematic literature review identified 10 RCTs and observa-
tional studies in patients with MM (n = 1026) who received primary
thromboprophylaxis with direct anti‐Xa agents or ASA. Thrombo-
prophylaxis with direct anti‐Xa agents was associated with a lower
incidence of VTE as compared to that with ASA (odds ratio [OR] 0.33;
95% CI 0.16–0.68; P < 0.001), whereas the bleeding events did
not differ significantly between the two groups.49 The authors re-
commend caution due to the heterogeneity of population char-
acteristics and potential bias of the observational studies included. An
exploratory meta‐analysis of studies in patients with MM investigated
thromboprophylaxis with ASA versus no intervention or prophylactic
dose of LWMH. Ten studies involving 1964 patients were analyzed.50
In several longitudinal studies and two RCTs, ASA reduced the risk of
VTE significantly compared to no intervention (OR = 0.20; 95% CI:
0.07–0.61; P = 0.005; I2 = 41%) but was associated with higher VTE
risk compared to LMWH (OR = 2.60; 95% CI: 1.08–6.25; P = 0.03;
I2 = 0%). An older prospective observational study (MELISSE trial),
which enrolled patients with MM treated with IMiDs, reported a 7%
VTE rate in patients who received ASA versus 3% in those treated
with LMWH and none in patients treated with VKA.38
Thromboprophylaxis with direct anti‐Xa agents in outpatients
with MM is an attractive strategy due to the convenient mode of ad-
ministration, the wide therapeutic window, and the limited drug‐to‐drug
interactions compared to VKA. The AVERT and CASSINI trials rando-
mized 574 and 841 intermediate/high‐risk patients for CAT (based on a
value of ≥2 on the Khorana risk score) to either placebo or apixaban
2.5 mg b.i.d. or rivaroxaban 10 mg o.d., respectively. In the AVERT trial,
apixaban 2.5 mg b.i.d. for 180 days was associated with a lower VTE rate
than placebo (4.2% vs. 10.2%; HR: 0.41 [95% CI 0.26–0.65; P = 0.0001];
number needed to treat = 17). The rate of major bleeding was higher in
patients in the apixaban arm (3.5% vs. 1.8%; HR 2.00 [95% CI
1.01–3.95; P = 0.046]; number needed to harm = 59).51 In the CASSINI
trial, administration of rivaroxaban 10 mg o.d. for 180 days did not show
a significant difference in VTE risk compared to placebo. Still, VTE rates
were significantly lower with rivaroxaban in the on‐treatment analysis
(2.6% vs. 6.4% HR 0.40 [95% CI 0.2–0.8]). The rate of major bleeding
was low and comparable in both groups.52 Neither study included pa-
tients with major thrombocytopenia (platelet count <50 × 109/L) or se-
vere renal impairment (creatinine clearance [CrCl] < 30 mL/min). Of
note, only a limited number of patients with MM were included in these
studies. Three pilot studies53‐55 and a retrospective one, enrolled MM
patients and showed promising results for the efficacy and safety of
thromboprophylaxis with apixaban 2.5 mg b.i.d. for at least 6 months.3
Among 305 patients, only two VTE events were reported (0.6%).
A retrospective study of 305 patients with NDMM showed that
thromboprophylaxis with rivaroxaban 10 mg o.d. can be an effective
and safe means of thromboprophylaxis.3 In patients treated with
KRD plus rivaroxaban, the VTE rate was 4.8% compared to 16.1%
in patients treated with ASA, similar to that of patients treated
with
VRD
and
ASA
(4.8%).
A
small
study
of
37
NDMM
patients or relapsed disease compared the efficacy and safety of
thromboprophylaxis with warfarin at doses adapted to achieve an in-
ternational normalized ratio (INR) of 2–3 or apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily
for 3 months. No VTE events were observed in the warfarin group. One
VTE episode was reported in the apixaban group, major hemorrhagic
episodes in two and four patients, respectively.56 Another study in 70
NDMM patients on IMiD‐based regimens reported no cases of VTE
among those receiving apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily. However, two ar-
terial events and one major bleeding episode were noted, highlighting
the need for ongoing evaluation of anticoagulation strategies in this
patient population.55 Overall, data point to equivalent efficacy and
safety of direct anti‐Xas compared to LWMH, which justifies the use of
either in this setting.
Nevertheless, there is a need for Phase III RCTs evaluating the
efficacy and safety of thromboprophylaxis with direct anti‐Xa agents
or LMWHs in high‐risk patients with MM.
Duration of thromboprophylaxis
With improvement in survival rates for MM patients, a greater focus
on the long‐term management of VTE risk is needed. NDMM patients
receive prolonged‐duration lenalidomide maintenance therapy fol-
lowing induction, and patients at relapse continue treatment until
disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. Regular evaluation of
the benefit–risk ratio of extended pharmacological thromboprophy-
laxis is therefore required. Lenalidomide maintenance carries a con-
tinuous VTE risk between 4% and 6%.57 A post hoc analysis of the
Griffin trial showed that VTE risk is highest at induction but continues
to increase over time (2.2 for daraVRD and 7% for VRD at main-
tenance between Cycles 7 and 18 and 3.6% and 1.7%, respectively, at
maintenance 18+).58 At subsequent treatment lines, even when the
tumor burden decreases, the VTE risk persists.
Key steps in optimizing the benefit–risk ratio of extended
pharmacological thromboprophylaxis in patients with MM include the
following:
i. Implement validated RAM (e.g., IMPEDE‐VTE) and regular
reassessment of VTE risk.
ii. Consider treatment‐specific risks (IMiDs, high‐dose dexamethasone,
etc.) versus.
iii. Regular reassessment of thrombotic/bleeding risks
• evaluation of bleeding risk predictors: anemia, thrombocyto-
penia, renal and liver function, and drug‐to‐drug interactions.
iv. Regular evaluation of patients' preference, patients' adherence,
and the impact of extended thromboprophylaxis on the quality‐
of‐life indexes.
v. Patient education on

• early recognition of symptoms and signs of VTE,
• early recognition of symptoms and signs of hemorrhagic
diathesis, and
• clear patients' pathway in case of bleeding or VTE.
Recommendations
1. We recommend to provide in patients with M with educational ma-
terials on VTE. These materials should include comprehensive details
on the burden of VTE and its consequences in patients with MM, on
the risk factors of VTE, the clinical signs and symptoms of VTE, a
guidance to enhance patient awareness as well as information for
communication with the treating physicians specialized for the man-
agement of VTE and antithrombotic treatment (Grade 1A).
2. We recommend the implementation of systematic VTE risk assess-
ment for all MM patients at the initial diagnosis, treatment in-
itiation, relapse/progression, and whenever treatment regimens
are modified (Grade 1A).
3. We recommend the implementation of the IMPEDE‐VTE score for
VTE risk assessment in all patients with MM; given its comprehen-
sive inclusion of relevant risk factors, applicability across treatment
regimens, and reliance on universally available clinical parameters.
The IMPEDE‐VTE score should be applied within the context of its
limitations (Grade 1B).
4. We recommend the stratification of patients into two risk groups as
either high/intermediate or low risk for VTE (Grade 2A).
5. Patients who receive IMiD‐based regimens or carfilzomib combina-
tions should be stratified as high/intermediate risk for VTE (Grade 2Β).
6. We suggest risk assessment for VTE upon primary diagnosis and
antimyeloma treatment initiation, following induction completion or
disease remission, at diagnosis of disease progression or recurrence,
when modifications of therapeutic lines are scheduled (Grade 2B).
7. We recommend the administration of pharmacological thrombo-
prophylaxis with direct anti‐Xa agents or LMWH to patients clas-
sified at high/intermediate risk for VTE (Grade 1B).
a. Antithrombotic agents suggested for thromboprophylaxis in high/
intermediate‐risk patients with MM are (see Table 4 for dosing
guidelines):
i. LMWH, o.d. subcutaneous injection in prophylactic doses
approved by health authorities and as recommended by the
manufacturer.
ii. Rivaroxaban 10 mg o.d. p.o.
iii. Apixaban 2.5 mg b.i.d. p.o.
iv. We recommend to consider apixaban 2.5 mg b.i.d. or rivar-
oxaban 10 mg o.d. as potential alternatives for patients with
CrCl > 30 mL/min who have contraindications or intolerance
to LMWH or a previous history of heparin‐induced thrombo-
cytopenia. In patients with a CrCl < 30 mL/min, LMWH with
dose adjustments (if required) is the preferred prophylaxis
(Grade 2B).
v. VKAs at doses adapted to obtain a target INR 2.0–3.0 are an
option if direct anti‐Xas are not available and LMWH are
contraindicated.
8. We recommend to evaluate the bleeding risk (considering platelet
count, hemoglobin levels, renal function, contraindications to
anticoagulation, drug–drug interactions, and personal history of
clinically relevant hemorrhage) in patients at high/intermediate risk
for VTE who are scheduled for pharmacological thromboprophylaxis
(Grade 1A).
9. We recommend the administration of low‐dose aspirin for patients
classified as low risk (Grade 2B).
10. We recommend applying pharmacological thromboprophylaxis for at
least 6 months from the last risk evaluation. The panel discourages
discontinuation of thromboprophylaxis particularly in patients with
residual disease receiving treatment with IMiD. Downgrading throm-
boprophylaxis during maintenance should be considered on a case‐by‐
case basis following risk assessment with the IMPEDE score and
careful evaluation of the benefit/risk ratio, patients' preference and
impact of the pharmacological thromboprophylaxis on the quality of
life of the patient.
11. The panel encourages research on the efficacy and safety of
thromboprophylaxis with LMWH or direct anti‐Xa and the con-
duction of Phase III studies with risk‐stratified patient selection.
12. The panel encourages research on the comparison of the existing
RAMs and the performance of appropriately designed prospective
cohort studies to derive and validate clinico‐biological RAMs for ac-
curate identification of MM patients eligible for thromboprophylaxis.
Thromboprophylaxis in acute medically ill patients
with MM
Patients with MM suffering acute medical illness, including COVID‐
19, benefit equally from the standard thromboprophylaxis practices
for acutely ill medical patients and should receive the same throm-
boprophylaxis as recommended in the most recent guidelines.
Recommendations
We
recommend
to
administer
prophylactic
doses
of
LMWH
or
fondaparinux to patients with active MM and acute medical illness,
either hospitalized or receiving care in the primary healthcare setting or
at home (Grade 1A).

header: Treatment of VTE in patients with MM
content:
LMWH has been the first choice for the acute phase of thrombosis in
patients with malignancies and for long‐term secondary prevention for
almost 2 decades.59 Phase III RCTs conducted in patients with CAT
showed that the direct anti‐Xa agents, apixaban, edoxaban, or rivarox-
aban, have similar efficacy and safety compared to LMWHs.60–63
The multicenter randomized double‐blind, noninferiority trial
APICAT compared the efficacy and safety of apixaban at a reduced
(2.5 mg) or full (5.0 mg) dose twice daily for 12 months (2.5 mg b.i.d.)
in 1766 patients with active cancer and proximal DVT or PE who
had completed at least 6 months of anticoagulant.64 The patients
with MM were 3.8% in the reduced‐dose group and 4.1% in the
full‐dose group. The median treatment duration was 11.8 months
(interquartile range, 8.3–12.1). Recurrent VTE occurred in 18 patients
(cumulative incidence, 2.1%) in the reduced‐dose group and in 24
(cumulative incidence, 2.8%) in the full‐dose group (adjusted sub‐HR,
0.76; 95% CI, 0.41–1.41; P = 0.001 for noninferiority). Clinically
relevant bleeding occurred in 102 patients (cumulative incidence,
12.1%) in the reduced‐dose group and in 136 (cumulative incidence,
15.6%) in the full‐dose group (adjusted sub‐HR, 0.75; 95% CI,
0.58–0.97; P = 0.03). Mortality was 17.7% in the reduced‐dose group
and 19.6% in the full‐dose group (adjusted HR, 0.96; 95% CI,
0.86–1.06). Extended anticoagulation with reduced‐dose apixaban
was noninferior to full‐dose apixaban for the prevention of recurrent VTE
in patients with active cancer. The reduced dose led to a lower incidence
of clinically relevant bleeding complications than the full dose.
Patients with MM were underrepresented in these clinical
trials60–62,65 but overall, VTE management follows the same patterns
as in other malignancies.
In selected patients (i.e., elderly and frail patients, patients with
renal insufficiency, and patients with liver dysfunction), treatment
with LMWH compared to direct anti‐Xa agents has the advantage of
dose adjustment based on either the body weight or the levels of the

injection. LMWH also presents limited drug‐to‐drug interactions,
particularly relevant when patients receive strong inhibitors or in-
ducers of the cytochrome P450 3A4 (CYP3A4) and P‐glycoprotein.
There are no data on the safety of the antithrombotic treatment
for secondary prevention of VTE in MM patients with renal impair-
ment. Post hoc and subgroup analyses suggest that LMWH and direct
factor Xa inhibitors in patients with moderate renal impairment (CrCl
30–60 mL/min) can be administered without dose modifications as
the safety and efficacy profile does not change. Patients with severe
renal impairment (CrCl < 30 mL/min) were excluded from the randomized
Phase III clinical trials, and data for anti‐Xa agents in this setting are
lacking. Therefore, either unfractionated heparin (UFH) followed by VKA
or LMWH with the dose adjusted to anti‐Xa activity level should be
applied. For patients with severe thrombocytopenia (<50 × 109/L), if there
is a high risk of thrombosis progression, full‐dose anticoagulation with
platelet transfusion support can be administered, or for lower risk cases,
intermediate or prophylactic dose LMWH is an option.
The optimal management of superficial vein thrombosis has not
been established yet, and data in MM patients are lacking. Superficial
vein thrombosis (SVT) of the long saphenous vein within 3 cm of the
saphenofemoral junction (SFJ) is considered equivalent to a DVT. For
less severe forms of SVT, the recommendation would be therapeutic
anticoagulation for 6 weeks at least and then switch to prophylaxis,
but there are no MM‐specific data to guide clinical practice.
Recommendations
i. Treatment of the acute phase of VTE in patients with MM.
1. We
recommend
a
systematic
evaluation
of
the
bleeding
risk before the initiation of antithrombotic therapy. Predictors
of bleeding risk are: renal insufficiency, low platelet count
(<50 G/L), anemia, and drug–drug interactions (for the direct
anti‐Xa agents) (Grade 1A).
2. We recommend, without interruption of the antimyeloma therapy,
a. the initiation of a therapeutic dose of LMWH in patients at
high bleeding risk (Grade 1A);
b. the initiation of therapeutic doses of apixaban, edoxaban, or
rivaroxaban in patients with low bleeding risk and low potential
of drug–drug interactions (edoxaban requires lead‐in therapy
with LMWH for the first 5–10 days) (Grade 1A);
c. the initiation of therapeutic doses of UFH with regular mon-
itoring of the anti‐Xa levels in plasma in patients with severe
renal insufficiency (CrCl < 30 mL/min) (Grade 1A).
3. We suggest, in the case of life‐threatening or recurrent VTE during
IMiD/carfilzomib‐based regimens, individualized therapeutic deci-
sions. Factors to be considered in individualized therapeutic
strategy include the depth of response to the current regimen,
the severity of the thrombotic event, and the patient's risk profile
recurrent VTE (Grade 2C).
ii. Secondary prevention of VTE in patients with MM.
1. We recommend for secondary prevention of VTE treatment
with LMWH or anti‐Xa agents (apixaban, edoxaban, or rivar-
oxaban) (Grade 1A).
a. The optimal antithrombotic agent should be selected based
on the renal function and the potential drug–drug interac-
tions (Grade 1B).
2. We recommend a minimum duration of 6 months for antith-
rombotic treatment in patients with VTE and MM (Grade 1A).
• Active MM is defined as (i) NDMM, (ii) MM at relapse/
progression, and (iii) MM disease that is not in remission
despite treatment administration.
3. We recommend extension of the antithrombotic treatment
beyond 6 months in patients with active MM (Grade 1A).
4. We recommend the continuation of the antithrombotic treat-
ment beyond 6 months in patients receiving ongoing IMiD or
carfilzomib therapy.
5. We recommend for patients with active MM that the decision
for
the
extension
of
antithrombotic
treatment
beyond
6 months should involve careful evaluation of VTE recurrence
risk versus bleeding risk (Grade 1A).
a. We suggest referring the patient to a thrombosis and
hemostasis specialist. Periodic reevaluation of thrombotic
and hemorrhagic risk is necessary during long‐term antith-
rombotic treatment (Grade 2B).
6. We
recommend
reduced‐dose
apixaban
(2.5 mg
twice
daily) over full‐dose apixaban (5 mg twice daily) for MM pa-
tients requiring extended secondary prevention of CAT beyond
6 months post‐index event (Grade 1B).
Prophylaxis and treatment of catheter‐related VTE
Evidence is scarce among cancer patients, and there are no data
specific for MM. There is one prospective trial that evaluated LMWH
plus VKA in cancer patients with catheter‐related VTE. There is no
robust data yet concerning the role of factor Xa inhibitors.
Recommendations
1. Routine pharmacological prophylaxis is not recommended for the
prevention of catheter‐related VTE (Grade 2C).
2. For symptomatic catheter‐related VTE in patients with MM, antic-
oagulation is recommended for a minimum of 3 months (Grade 2a).
The preferred first‐line therapy is LMWH, but anti‐Xa agents or VKAs
are alternative options (Grade 2C).
3. We recommend antithrombotic treatment for a minimum of 3 months
for symptomatic catheter‐related VTE in patients with MM, antic-
oagulation (Grade 2A). The preferred first‐line therapy is LMWH, with
direct oral anti‐Xa agents or VKAs as alternative options (Grade 2C).
4. We suggest evaluation of the risk of myeloma‐associated VTE after
3 months of the antithrombotic treatment and application of the
recommended thromboprophylaxis if the patient is stratified at high/
intermediate risk (Grade 2C).
5. Overall, further MM‐specific recommendations cannot be extrapolated.
The panel recommends that these patients are managed according to
standard clinical practice for non‐MM patients and encourages the design
of trials that could provide data regarding CVC use in MM patients.

The EMN‐VTE Consensus Guidelines mark a significant advancement
in VTE management for MM patients. These recommendations were
developed through rigorous multidisciplinary collaboration of experts
in thrombosis and specialists in MM, under the auspices of the EMN.
This expert consensus provides tailored, evidence‐based strategies
that address the unique thrombotic risks inherent to myeloma
pathophysiology and its treatments.
This document introduces key updates, including the simplifica-
tion of risk stratification by grouping intermediate‐ and high‐risk
patients, and advocates for the use of factor Xa inhibitors alongside
LWMH for primary thromboprophylaxis. The panel recommends
universal thromboprophylaxis for all newly diagnosed or relapsed
patients initiating treatment with IMiDs or carfilzomib combinations,
regardless of baseline risk scores, following a careful hemorrhagic
risk evaluation. This recommendation is supported by data showing
persistently high VTE rates in patients receiving IMiDs, even when
stratified by risk models, and recognizes that current risk assessment
10 of 14
|
tools were developed before the use of carfilzomib. By integrating
disease activity and contemporary treatment regimens into its
framework, the EMN‐VTE guideline addresses unmet clinical needs
and aims to harmonize and improve real‐world thromboprophylaxis
practices.
The key points addressed by the Expert Panel Consensus
Guidance are as follows:
1. VTE is a frequent and life‐threatening complication of MM that
negatively impacts patients' quality of life, interferes with the
scheduled treatment, and decreases life expectancy.
2. Hematologists, angiologists, thrombosis, and hemostasis experts
and general practitioners should do the following:
• Inform patients about the risk of VTE, educate them about
the clinical signs and symptoms, target modifiable risk factors,
and inform them about the pathways for prompt diagnosis and
treatment.
• Perform routine and regular evaluation of VTE risk by applying
recommended RAMs, acknowledging that the accuracy of
these scores is suboptimal.
• Evaluate regularly the bleeding risk, acknowledging that a
validated RAM for bleeding risk in ambulatory cancer patients
eligible for pharmacological thromboprophylaxis is not yet
available.
• Initiate, for intermediate and high‐risk patients, pharmacological
thromboprophylaxis with LMWH or factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban
or rivaroxaban) at the recommended prophylactic doses.
• Initiate low‐dose ASA in patients classified as low risk.
• Initiate
pharmacological
thromboprophylaxis
with
LMWH
or factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban or rivaroxaban) at the
recommended prophylactic doses for all patients due to start
IMiD‐based or carfilzomib‐based therapy.
• Pharmacological thromboprophylaxis should be administered
for at least 6 months, followed by a case‐by‐case evaluation.
• Predefine
a
local
healthcare
pathway—from
the
primary
healthcare structures up to specialized academic centers—for
prompt diagnosis and treatment of VTE in ambulatory patients
with MM.
• Initiate antithrombotic treatment with therapeutic doses of
LMWH or factor Xa inhibitors in patients with diagnosed VTE
for at least 6 months.
• Privilege low‐dose apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily over full‐dose
apixaban (5 mg twice daily) for patients eligible for long‐term
secondary prevention of VTE after 6 months from the index
event.
• Apply a personalized strategy for the long‐term anticoagulation
for secondary prevention of VTE taking into consideration the
disease status, hematological response, and bleeding risks.
• The choice of the optimal antithrombotic agent, either for
primary thromboprophylaxis or VTE treatment, should take into
consideration patients' preference, impact on quality of life, and
drug‐to‐drug interactions.
Given the complexity of managing patients with MM, whether at
risk of VTE or already diagnosed with VTE, along with the dynamic
nature of disease progression, evolving treatment regimens, and
the aging population, the panel strongly recommends establishing
specialized outpatient Thrombosis Consultations. We require more
effective and updated VTE risk assessment tools, developed pro-
spectively based on cohorts that include triplet/quadruplet novel
agent combinations to reflect current clinical practice accurately.
In addition, prospective randomized trials are required to provide
answers to the question of optimal thromboprophylaxis modality and
duration, based on MM‐specific patient profiles. VTE is likely to re-
main an ongoing risk for MM patients, and as a community, we need
to implement consistent clinical practices to improve outcomes.
